one_line_takeaway: A system-level survey arguing that AI-native semantic communication needs cross-layer, deployment-aware security defenses (spanning encoder/decoder, channel, knowledge base, and multi-agent inference) because standalone-model AI-security techniques do not account for SemCom's coupled learning-transmission-inference pipeline.
key_finding: The paper's central claim is a gap-identification finding, not an empirical result: existing SemCom-security surveys each cover only a subset of the security surfaces the authors define (per Table I, no prior survey covers all of Semantic Model + Wireless Channel + Shared Knowledge + Semantic Inference + Networked Inference simultaneously), and existing AI-security techniques (adversarial training, data-poisoning defenses, membership-inference protection, secure federated learning) are 'primarily designed for standalone models and rarely account for deployment within dynamic, resource-constrained, over-the-air communication pipelines,' so isolated model vulnerabilities can propagate into system-level SemCom failures unaddressed by existing frameworks. The survey's synthesized argument is that securing SemCom must be treated as a cross-layer, system-level co-design problem (spanning encoding, transmission, knowledge integrity, and multi-agent coordination) governed by explicit security-utility operating envelopes, rather than as isolated model hardening.
core_contribution: A defense-centered, system-level survey of security in AI-native semantic communication (SemCom) systems. The paper's own contribution (per its stated bullets in Sec. I.B) is fourfold: (1) an AI-centric threat model that consolidates SemCom- and AI-security literature into a taxonomy of attack surfaces spanning model-centric, channel-realizable, knowledge-based, and networked-inference vectors; (2) a layered defense taxonomy organized by where semantic integrity can be compromised (encoder/decoder, wireless channel, knowledge base, network/multi-agent level); (3) a deployment-oriented framing of 'security-utility operating envelopes' that consolidates how the literature analyzes tradeoffs among semantic fidelity, robustness, latency, energy, and adversarial budget; and (4) an audit of evaluation/benchmarking gaps in existing SemCom security studies. The paper explicitly differentiates itself from prior SemCom-security surveys (Table I lists Xin et al. 2024, Guo et al. 2024, Won et al. 2025, Li et al. 2024, Meng et al. 2025, Yang et al. 2024, Shen et al. 2023) by claiming to be the first to jointly cover all five surfaces the authors define: Semantic Model, Wireless Channel, Shared Knowledge, Semantic Inference, and Networked Inference.
threat_model: The paper frames SemCom as AI-native: semantic encoders/decoders and knowledge-access modules are learned models whose behavior depends on training data quality, generalization, robustness, and context alignment, so semantic failures can occur even when lower-layer bit-level delivery and cryptographic protection remain intact (Table II). It organizes three underlying security concerns: (a) dependence on complex learned models -- adversarial input manipulation against semantic encoders can induce misleading latent representations without violating channel-level integrity; (b) dependence on data-driven learning corpora -- data poisoning or federated desynchronization can gradually shift the encoder/decoder mapping (semantic drift) even with bit-level fidelity preserved; (c) dependence on external semantic resources -- tampering with shared foundation models, embeddings, or knowledge bases (ontology manipulation / prior-mismatch injection) can create endpoint mismatches and ambiguous decoding despite error-free symbol delivery. A fuller, formalized AI-centric threat model (adversarial objectives, attacker capabilities/access assumptions, and attack classes: model-centric threats, channel-realizable semantic attacks, semantic manipulation via knowledge/context, and networked/multi-agent propagation) is developed in Section IV, which synthesizes and organizes prior work rather than proposing new attacks of its own.
limitations: As a survey, the paper's own scope limits (stated or evident) include: it explicitly excludes access-network- and network-level architectures from its architectural discussion (Section II), focusing only on AI-based semantic transceivers and knowledge-assisted architectures. It identifies but does not resolve open gaps in its own domain: lack of standardized evaluation metrics/benchmarks for SemCom security, absence of reproducible adversarial testbeds, and unresolved challenges in cross-layer composition and deployment-time certification (Section VIII, per abstract). Because it is a synthesis rather than an empirical contribution, it offers no independently validated quantitative evidence of its own; all effectiveness/robustness numbers referenced in the text originate from third-party cited studies and are not verified or reproduced by the authors.
